content:
6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years ago I had two friends chasing the same goal. Both wanted to learn programming and eventually turn it into a career.
The first one was the motivated type — the kind of motivated that's almost exhausting to be around. He watched inspirational videos most mornings, bought three or four online courses he never finished, and filled a notebook with plans for the life he was going to build. He talked about it a lot.
The second friend barely mentioned it. No big announcements, no motivational quotes. He just quietly rearranged a few things. He cleared off his desk. He started leaving his phone in the kitchen while he studied. He found an online community of other people learning to code, mostly so he'd have somewhere to ask dumb questions without feeling stupid. Then, most evenings, he sat in the same chair and put in an hour.
A year later there wasn't really a competition anymore. The first friend was still waiting to feel ready. The second had already landed his first freelance gig.
It took me a while to understand what actually happened there. It wasn't that one friend wanted it more — if anything, the first one talked about wanting it more. The difference was that the second friend had built an environment where the work was the easy option, and the first hadn't.
The Invisible Force Behind Every Habit
We like to think our choices come from pure willpower, but most of the time our surroundings are quietly doing the deciding for us. Walk into a library and you drop your voice without thinking about it. Walk past a bakery and suddenly you're hungry, even though you ate an hour ago. Nobody's forcing that — the environment just nudges you there.
Your habits work the same way. A messy desk makes it easier to get distracted. A phone buzzing every few minutes makes procrastination the path of least resistance. Spend enough time around people who complain about everything, and complaining starts to feel like the normal way to talk. None of this requires your permission. It just happens in the background, all day, whether you're paying attention or not.
Why Motivation Runs Out
Motivation feels great while it lasts. For a day or two, you're convinced you can rebuild your entire life before lunch. Then you wake up on a Tuesday and can barely get out of bed, and the plan from Sunday night suddenly seems like it belonged to someone else.
That's the problem with relying on it — motivation is a mood, and moods are unreliable. If your progress depends on how you feel that morning, it's going to be inconsistent, because how you feel that morning is inconsistent. This is basically why so many people start strong in January and have quietly given up by February. It's not a lack of willpower. It's that they never built anything sturdier than a feeling.
The people who keep going for years, not weeks, usually aren't more disciplined in some innate way. They've just set things up so the good habit is the easier habit, most days, even when they don't particularly feel like it.
Small Changes Do the Heavy Lifting
Say you want to read more. You could wait to feel like it every night — good luck — or you could just leave the book on your pillow before you go to work. Now it's sitting there when you get home, before your brain has had a chance to talk you out of it.
Same logic applies almost everywhere. Want to eat better? Put fruit on the counter where you'll actually see it, and put the chips somewhere annoying to reach. Trying to cut down on your phone? Delete the one or two apps that eat most of your time — you can always redownload them, but that five-second friction is often enough. Want to work out before work instead of after? Lay your clothes out the night before so getting dressed for the gym doesn't require a decision at 6 a.m.
None of this sounds impressive. That's kind of the point — the changes work precisely because they're boring. They don't ask you to be a different person; they just remove a few of the small decisions that used to give your excuses room to breathe.
Your Environment Includes the People In It
It's not just your room or your desk. The people you spend time with are part of your environment too, and honestly probably the biggest part.
If everyone around you treats ambition as something to roll their eyes at, it gets harder to take your own goals seriously — even privately. On the other hand, being around people who are curious, who actually enjoy figuring things out, tends to raise your own bar without anyone saying a word about it.
Pay attention to how you feel after spending time with different people. Some conversations leave you wanting to go do something. Others leave you a little flatter than before. That's not random — it's the people setting the tone, and you absorbing it more than you'd probably like to admit.
This Isn't Tied to Where You Live
I've heard versions of this same story from people in pretty different situations. A student in Lahore whose house is too loud and crowded to study in starts going to the library instead, or studies late at night once everyone else is asleep. A friend in Toronto started leaving her phone in another room during work hours and was shocked by how much faster she got through her to-do list. Someone I know in London joined a small networking group of other founders, mostly just to have people around who wouldn't laugh if he said something ambitious out loud. A freelancer I follow online finally set up an actual desk instead of working from the couch and said it changed how seriously he took his own workday.
Different cities, different problems — but the same fix underneath all of them. Either your surroundings are quietly pulling you toward your goals, or they're quietly pulling you away, and most people never stop to check which one is happening.
Designing an Environment That Works For You
You don't need a full life overhaul or a Pinterest-worthy setup. Start smaller than that.
Clear off your desk. Turn off the notifications that don't actually matter. Spend a bit more time around the people who make you want to try harder, and a bit less around the ones who don't. Put something in your line of sight that reminds you what you're working toward.
The general idea is just this: make the good habit slightly easier to do, and the bad habit slightly more annoying to reach. Do that consistently and discipline stops being the main event — it just becomes the natural result of the setup.
Final Thought
A lot of people spend years waiting for motivation that never quite arrives on schedule. They watch the videos, save the quotes, keep waiting for the right mood to show up and carry them through.
But the people who actually build something usually did it more quietly than that. They didn't wait to feel ready — they just made the right choice slightly easier than the wrong one, over and over, until it stopped requiring much thought at all.
So look around you right now. Your desk. Your phone. The people you've talked to most this week. Then ask yourself, honestly — is any of that helping you become who you're trying to be?
If it's not, you don't need more motivation. You need a different environment. Sometimes changing one small thing around you is what actually changes something inside you.
